title: Vaccine Glossary | Vaccines & Immunizations | CDC
text: example, maternal antibodies are passed to the infant prior to birth and protect the baby for the first 4–6 months of life. Pathogens Organisms (such as bacteria, viruses, parasites and fungi) that cause disease in human beings. Pertussis Listen Bacterial infectious disease marked by a convulsive spasmodic cough, sometimes followed by a crowing intake of breath. Also known as whooping cough. Learn more . Petechiae Listen A tiny reddish or purplish spot on the skin or mucous membrane, commonly part of infectious diseases such as typhoid fever. Placebo A substance or treatment that has no effect on living beings, usually used as a comparison to vaccine or medicine in clinical trials. Pneumonia Listen Inflammation of the lungs characterized by fever, chills, muscle stiffness, chest pain, cough, shortness of breath, rapid heart rate, and difficulty breathing. Poliomyelitis Listen An acute infectious viral disease characterized by fever, paralysis, and atrophy of skeletal muscles. Also known as polio. Learn more . Polysaccharide vaccines Listen Vaccines composed of long chains of sugar molecules that resemble the surface of certain types of bacteria. Polysaccharide vaccines are available for pneumococcal disease. Potency A measure of strength. Precaution A condition in a recipient that might increase their risk for a serious adverse reaction, might cause diagnostic confusion,
